title: Journey's fifth door: Memory graph (wiki) — opens the generated wiki graph.html in the browser, private overlay wiki first
context: The wiki gained an interactive memory-graph page (wiki/graph.html — components, dependencies, compounding-replay time scrubber). The owner asked for it in Journey's calls to action so the story screen links to the map.
decision: Journey's CTA-door contract stands, with a fifth allowlisted door: hunch.memoryGraph. The command is a pure opener — it resolves the generated wiki's graph.html (the private overlay wiki wins when it exists since it renders the full union and never leaves the machine; else the public wiki; wiki dir read from wiki-manifest.json, defaulting to 'wiki') and opens it externally in the browser. When no graph exists it says exactly how to generate one (hunch wiki [--private]) instead of generating anything itself — generation stays a deliberate CLI act. The full allowlist is now hunch.capture, hunch.why, hunch.search, hunch.reviewInTerminal, hunch.memoryGraph; everything else in the door contract is unchanged. v0.12.0.
consequences: The Journey story links to the interactive map; both compounding surfaces (curve + graph) are one click apart.
hunch.memoryGraph is visible in the palette as 'Memory graph (wiki)'.
The extension still never generates or writes wiki pages — it only opens what the CLI produced.
alternatives_rejected: Render the memory graph inside a webview panel — rejected: graph.html is a self-contained page owned by the wiki generator; embedding would fork its lifecycle away from the manifest/tamper/heal machinery.
Auto-run `hunch wiki` when the page is missing — rejected: generation can invoke the subscription CLI and takes time; a door that silently spawns work stops being a door.
Public wiki preferred over private — rejected: the private overlay wiki is the fuller, safer view on the owner's machine; the public copy is the fallback.
